title: eol_hardware_failure
description: End-of-life hardware fails with no vendor support available, requiring an emergency migration.

timeline.title: Legacy Server Failure
timeline.description: A server that reached end-of-life 2 years ago fails. The hardware is no longer supported by the vendor.

timeline.title: No Vendor Support Available
timeline.description: Support ticket opened with vendor is immediately rejected. Hardware is past EOL with no spare parts available.

timeline.title: Application Inventory Review
timeline.description: Teams scramble to document what applications were running on the EOL server.

timeline.title: Emergency Provisioning
timeline.description: New virtual or cloud infrastructure is provisioned as an emergency replacement.

timeline.title: Application Migration
timeline.description: Applications are migrated and reconfigured on the new infrastructure.

timeline.title: Services Restored
timeline.description: All workloads are operational on new infrastructure. EOL hardware decommissioned.

node-1.title: EOL Server Has Failed
node-1.situation: A server that has been past vendor end-of-life for 2 years has failed with a motherboard fault. No spare parts exist. The server hosts 3 business-critical applications. The CMDB entry for this server is incomplete and the applications on it are not well documented.
node-1.question: What is your immediate action?

options.label: A
options.text: Declare a P1 incident, notify application owners to identify running workloads from logs and documentation, and begin provisioning emergency replacement infrastructure in parallel.

options.label: B
options.text: Spend time searching for third-party refurbished parts before taking any other action.

options.label: C
options.text: Restore a backup to the same failing hardware without addressing the underlying hardware fault.

node-2.title: Application Discovery
node-2.situation: Application owners have identified the 3 workloads: a legacy CRM, a file share, and a batch processing job. Only the CRM has current documentation. The file share and batch job configs must be recovered from disk backups of the failed server.
node-2.question: How do you proceed?

options.label: A
options.text: Mount the failed server disk in a recovery mode environment to retrieve application configs and data, while provisioning new VM infrastructure for all three workloads.

options.label: B
options.text: Only restore the documented CRM application and postpone the others until full documentation is available.

node-3.title: Emergency Migration
node-3.situation: Application configs and data have been recovered. New VMs are provisioned. You need to migrate the applications and validate them before directing users back to the services.
node-3.question: What do you do?

options.label: A
options.text: Migrate each application to the new VMs, run smoke tests for each service, update DNS records, and confirm with application owners that functionality is restored before closing.

options.label: B
options.text: Update DNS to point to the new VMs before testing the applications, to restore access as quickly as possible.

node-4.title: Post-Incident Hardening
node-4.situation: All three applications are migrated and operational. This incident exposed a gap: multiple EOL servers still exist in the datacenter. Management asks what should be done to prevent this from happening again.
node-4.question: What is your next step?

options.label: A
options.text: Conduct a full EOL hardware audit, create a prioritized replacement roadmap with business impact assessments, update the CMDB, and establish an EOL monitoring process with proactive alerts before EOL dates.

options.label: B
options.text: Replace EOL hardware only as it fails, since proactive replacement is too costly to justify.

node-5.title: Scenario Complete
node-5.situation: You managed a complex EOL hardware failure with incomplete documentation, successfully recovered application data, migrated all workloads to new infrastructure, and initiated a proactive EOL replacement program.
node-5.question: Scenario complete. Well done!
